{{Short description|Feeling or attitude in acknowledgement of a benefit that one has received or will receive}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=September 2021}}
{{About|the emotion}}
[[Image:® LISBOA MONUMENTO PALACIO DE SAO BENTO - panoramio - Concepcion AMAT ORTA… (2).jpg|thumb|upright=1.2|Gratitude. Statue in [[Palácio Nacional da Ajuda]].]]{{emotion}}

'''Gratitude''', '''thankfulness''', or '''gratefulness''' is a feeling of appreciation (or similar positive response<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Stewart McKay |first=William John |date=1922 |title=Lawson Tait, His Life and Work |journal=A Contribution to the History of Abdominal Surgery and Gynaecology |publisher=Wood |pages=26}}</ref>) by a recipient of another's kindness. This kindness can be gifts<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Barlow Felt |first=Joseph |date=1870 |title=New-England Historical and Genealogical Register and Antiquarian Journal |journal=New-England Historical and Genealogical Register and Antiquarian Journal |pages=260}}</ref>, help, favors, or another form of generosity to another person.

The word comes from the [[Latin]] word {{lang|la|gratus}}, which means "pleasing" or "thankful".<ref>{{citation|title=Definition of Gratitude|website=Oxford Dictionary|url=https://en.oxforddictionaries.com/definition/gratitude|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160924222506/https://en.oxforddictionaries.com/definition/gratitude|archive-date=24 September 2016}}</ref>  The absence of gratitude where gratitude is expected is called '''ingratitude'''<ref>{{cite book|first=John|last=Bate|title=A Cyclopædia of Illustrations of Moral and Religious Truths|year=1865|page=460}}</ref> or '''ungratefulness'''.<ref>{{cite book|first=Noah|last=Webster|title=An American Dictionary of the English Language|year=1854|page=542}}</ref>

Gratitude has been a part of several world [[religion]]s.<ref name="ReferenceB">{{cite journal|last1=Emmons|first1=Robert A.|last2=Crumpler|first2=Cheryl A.|year=2000|title=Gratitude as a Human Strength: Appraising the Evidence|journal=Journal of Social and Clinical Psychology|volume=19|issue=1|pages=56–69|doi=10.1521/jscp.2000.19.1.56}}</ref> It also has been a topic of interest to ancient, medieval, and modern [[philosophy|philosophers]].<ref name=manela>{{cite web|last=Manela|first=Tony|year=2019|url=https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/gratitude/|title=Gratitude|website=Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy|editor-first=Edward N.|editor-last=Zalta|access-date=14 May 2024}}</ref>

The discipline of psychology attempts to understand the short term experience of gratitude (state gratitude), individual differences in how frequently gratitude is felt ([[trait theory|trait]] gratitude), the relationship between these two, and the therapeutic benefits of gratitude.<ref>{{multiref2
|1={{cite journal|last1=Wood|first1=A. M.|last2=Maltby|first2=J.|last3=Stewart|first3=N.|last4=Linley|first4=P. A.|last5=Joseph|first5=S.|year=2008|title=A social-cognitive model of trait and state levels of gratitude|url=http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/state%20and%20trait%20gratitude.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180612140533/http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/state%20and%20trait%20gratitude.pdf|archive-date=2018-06-12|journal=Emotion|volume=8|issue=2|pages=281–290|doi=10.1037/1528-3542.8.2.281|pmid=18410201}}
|2={{cite journal | last1=McCullough | first1=Michael E. | last2=Tsang | first2=Jo-Ann | last3=Emmons | first3=Robert A. | title=Gratitude in Intermediate Affective Terrain: Links of Grateful Moods to Individual Differences and Daily Emotional Experience. | journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | publisher=American Psychological Association (APA) | volume=86 | issue=2 | year=2004 | issn=1939-1315 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.86.2.295 | pages=295–309| pmid=14769085}}
}}</ref>

==Philosophical approaches==
Gratitude is a topic of interest in the philosophical disciplines of [[normative ethics]], [[applied ethics]], and [[political philosophy]], as well as in the field of [[moral psychology]].<ref name=manela />

A. D. M. Walker suggests, contrary to views that only characterize gratitude as a kind of reciprocity, that gratitude is appropriate even toward an unintentional kindness, such as an offhand remark from a stranger. Walker further observes that [[Thomas Aquinas]] commended gratefulness for what benefits one even when done from contempt, reluctance and regret.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Walker |first1=A. D. M. |date=1981 |title=Gratefulness and Gratitude |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/4544964 |journal=Proceedings of the Aristotelian Society |volume=81 |publisher=Oxford University Press |pages=43-44 |access-date=11 May 2025}}</ref>

== Religious approaches ==
{{See also|Filial piety in Buddhism#Repaying the debt of gratitude}}
[[Spirituality]] and gratitude are not dependent on each other, but studies have found that spirituality can enhance a person's ability to be grateful. Those who regularly attend religious services or engage in religious activities are more likely to have a greater sense of gratitude in all areas of life.<ref name="McCullough, M.E. 2002">{{cite journal |last1=McCullough |first1=M.E. |last2=Emmons |first2=R.A. |last3=Tsang |first3=J. |year=2002 |title=The grateful disposition: A conceptual and empirical topography |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=83 |issue=1 |pages=112–127 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.82.1.112 |pmid=11811629}}</ref><ref name=Highlights>{{cite web |url=http://psychology.ucdavis.edu/labs/emmons/ |title=Highlights from the Research Project on Gratitude and Thankfulness |last1=Emmons|first1=Robert A. |first2=Michael E. |last2=McCullough |access-date=27 August 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100811213042/http://psychology.ucdavis.edu/labs/emmons/ |archive-date=11 August 2010}}</ref> Gratitude is prized in the [[Christianity|Christian]], [[Buddhism|Buddhist]], [[Islam|Muslim]], [[Judaism|Jewish]], [[Baháʼí]], and [[Hinduism|Hindu]] traditions.<ref name="ReferenceB"/> Worship with gratitude to [[God]],<ref>{{Cite book|last=Calvin|first=John|year=1845|orig-date=1557|title=Commentary on the Book of Psalms|volume=1|chapter=Commentary Upon Psalm VI|publisher=Calvin Translation Society|page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=E5g3w8x2t34C&pg=PA70 70]|translator-first=James|translator-last=Anderson|location=Edinburgh}}</ref> or a similar religious figure, is a common theme in such religions, and the concept of gratitude permeates religious texts, teachings, and traditions. It is one of the most common emotions that religions aim to evoke and maintain in followers and is regarded as a universal religious sentiment.<ref name="ReferenceA">{{cite journal |last1=Emmons |first1=Robert A. |last2=Kneezel |first2=Teresa T. |year=2005 |title=Giving Gratitude: Spiritual and Religious Correlates of Gratitude |journal=Journal of Psychology and Christianity |volume=24 |issue=2 |pages=140–48}}</ref>

=== Jewish conceptions ===
[[Image:Brockhaus and Efron Jewish Encyclopedia e4 183-1.jpg|thumb|230px|Berachot — thanksgiving and praise prayers. Illustration from the Jewish Encyclopedia of Brockhaus and Efron (1906–1913).]]
In Judaism, gratitude is an essential part of the act of worship and every aspect of a worshipper's life. According to the Hebrew worldview, all things come from God and, due to this, gratitude is essential to the followers of Judaism.

The Hebrew Scriptures are filled with the idea of gratitude. Two examples in the Psalms are "O Lord my God, I will give thanks to you forever", and "I will give thanks to the Lord with my whole heart."<ref>{{Bibleverse|Psalms|30:12}}, {{Bibleverse|Psalms|9:1}}</ref> Jewish prayers often incorporate gratitude, beginning with the Shema, in which the worshipper states that out of gratitude, "You shall love the Eternal, your God, with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your might."<ref>{{Bibleverse|Deuteronomy|6:5}}</ref> One of the crucial blessings in the central thrice-daily prayer, the Amidah, is called Modim – "We give thanks to You"; this is also the only blessing that is recited by the congregation together with the leader during their repetition of the Amidah. The concluding prayer, the Alenu, also speaks of gratitude by thanking God for the particular destiny of the Jewish people. Along with these prayers, faithful worshippers recite more than one hundred blessings, called {{transliteration|he|berachot}}, throughout the day.<ref name="ReferenceB"/>

In Judaism there is also a significant emphasis on gratitude for acts of human kindness and goodness.

=== Christian conceptions ===
[[File:GuadalupeNOLA15Oct07Thanks.jpg|thumb|right|230px|[[New Orleans]]: Thank you message in the grotto of [[Our Lady of Guadalupe]] Church; added by those for whom prayer or miracles were granted]]
[[Martin Luther]] referred to gratitude as "the basic Christian attitude" and today it is still referred to as "the heart of the gospel".<ref name="ReferenceA"/> Christians are encouraged to praise and give gratitude to their creator. In Christian gratitude, God is seen as the selfless giver of all good things and, because of this, gratefulness enables Christians to share a common bond, shaping all aspects of a follower's life. Gratitude in Christianity is an acknowledgement of God's generosity that inspires Christians to shape their own thoughts and actions around such{{specify|date=July 2023}} ideals.<ref name="ReferenceB"/>

Not simply a sentimental feeling, Christian gratitude is a virtue that shapes not only emotions and thoughts, but also actions and deeds.<ref name="ReferenceA"/>

[[Jonathan Edwards (theologian)|Jonathan Edwards]] wrote in his book ''A Treatise Concerning Religious Affections'' that gratitude and thankfulness toward God are among the signs of true religion. Allport (1950)<ref>{{Cite book |last=Allport |first=Gordon Willard |title=The Individual and His Religion: A Psychological Interpretation |date=1950 |publisher=Macmillan}}</ref> suggested that mature religious intentions come from feelings of profound gratitude; Edwards (1746/1959)<ref>{{cite book |last=Edwards |first=Jonathan |title=A Treatise Concerning Religious Affections |year=1959 |edition=Reprint |publisher=Baker Books |location=Grand Rapids, MI |url=https://ccel.org/ccel/edwards/affections |access-date=March 8, 2025}}</ref> claimed that the "affection" of gratitude is one of the most accurate ways to find the presence of God in a person's life. In a small sample of Catholic nuns and priests, out of 50 emotions, love and gratitude were the most experienced emotions towards God.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Samuels|first1=P.A.|last2=Lester|first2=D.|year=1985|title=A preliminary investigation of emotions experienced toward God by Catholic nuns and priests|journal=Psychological Reports|volume=56|issue=3 |page=706|doi=10.2466/pr0.1985.56.3.706 |s2cid=144205296 }}</ref><ref name="ReferenceB"/> 

[[Pope Francis]] has noted that one of the lessons generally learned in [[family life]] is learning to say "thank you" as "an expression of genuine gratitude for 
what we have been given".<ref>Pope Francis, [https://www.vatican.va/content/dam/francesco/pdf/encyclicals/documents/papa-francesco_20150524_enciclica-laudato-si_en.pdf Laudato si'], paragraph 213, published 24 May 2015, accessed 14 May 2024</ref>

In the Orthodox, Catholic, Lutheran, and Anglican churches, the most important rite is called the [[Eucharist]]; the name derives from the Greek word ''Eucharistic'', meaning thanksgiving.<ref name="Seiss1896">{{cite journal |last1=Seiss |first1=Joseph A. |title=The Liturgical Question|journal=The Quarterly Review of the Evangelical Lutheran Church|volume=26 |date=1896 |page=318 |language=en}}</ref>

=== Islamic conceptions ===
The Islamic sacred text, the [[Quran]], is filled with the idea of gratitude. Islam encourages Muslims to be grateful and to express thanks to Allah in all circumstances. Muslims commonly express their gratitude using the term "Alhamdulillah" (praise be to God), which is one of the four beloved words of Allah.

Even some verses of Quran indicates that, our main duty on this earth is to show our gratitude. For example, 
{{quote|Then I (satan) will come upon them from the front and from the rear, and from their right and from their left. And You will not find most of them thankful.|(Quran 7:17)}}
In this verse satan is saying to Allah that He will not find most humans grateful toward Him after when he (satan) refused to prostrate Adam. And the term [[Kafir]] also means 'ungrateful' (toward God) with the most fundamental sense in Quran.
Here are some other verses from Quran:
{{quote|Thus We (Allah) punished them because of their ungratefulness. We do not give (such a) punishment but to the ungrateful.|(Quran 34:17)}}

{{quote|We (Allah) already showed them the Way, whether they ˹choose to˺ be grateful or ungrateful.|(Quran 76:3)}}

Islamic teaching emphasizes that those who are grateful will be rewarded with more. A traditional Islamic saying is "The first who will be summoned to paradise are those who have praised God in every circumstance."<ref name="Gratitude - Parent of all virtues">{{cite journal |first1=Alex|last1= Wood|first2=Stephen|last2=Joseph|first3=Alex|last3=Linley |title=Gratitude — Parent of all virtues |journal=The Psychologist |date=January 2007 |volume=20 |issue=1 |pages=18–21 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/279558245 |access-date=12 March 2022}}</ref> The Quran also states, in Sura 14, that those who are grateful will be given more by Allah.

Many practices of Islam encourage gratitude. For example, the Pillar of Islam that calls for daily prayer encourages believers to pray to Allah five times a day in order to thank him for his goodness, and the pillar of fasting during the month of Ramadan is for the purpose of putting the believer in a state of gratitude.<ref name="ReferenceB"/>

== Individual differences in gratitude ==
Cultural and linguistic factors play a significant role in shaping expressions of gratitude worldwide.<ref>{{Cite web |title=How Cultural Differences Influence Gratitude Development |url=https://linnk.ai/insight/psychology/How-Cultural-Differences-Influence-Gratitude-Development-6xHLg89i/insight/psychology/How-Cultural-Differences-Influence-Gratitude-Development-6xHLg89i/ |access-date=2024-05-18 |website=linnk.ai |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Altakhaineh |first1=Abdel Rahman Mitib |last2=Younes |first2=Afakh Said |last3=Allawama |first3=Ashraf |date=2024-12-31 |title=A corpus-driven study of gratitude in English acknowledgements by Arabic-speaking MA students: constructing L2 academic writer identity |journal=Cogent Arts & Humanities |language=en |volume=11 |issue=1 |doi=10.1080/23311983.2024.2346361 |issn=2331-1983|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Bale |first1=Richard |last2=Pazio Rossiter |first2=Monika |date=2023-07-03 |title=The role of cultural and linguistic factors in shaping feedback practices: the perspectives of international higher education teaching staff |journal=Journal of Further and Higher Education |language=en |volume=47 |issue=6 |pages=810–821 |doi=10.1080/0309877X.2023.2188179 |issn=0309-877X|doi-access=free }}</ref> Much research into gratitude focuses on individual differences in gratitude and the consequences of being a more or less grateful person.<ref name="Gratitude - Parent of all virtues" /><ref>{{multiref2
|1={{cite book |last1=Emmons |first1=Robert A.|chapter=The assessment of gratitude. |year=2003 |chapter-url=https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2003-02181-021 |title=Positive psychological assessment: A handbook of models and measures. |pages=327–341 |editor-last1=Lopez |editor-first1=Shane J.|place=Washington |publisher=American Psychological Association |language=en |doi=10.1037/10612-021 |isbn=978-1-55798-988-8 |access-date=2023-02-10 |last2=McCullough |first2=Michael E. |last3=Tsang |first3=Jo-Ann |editor2-last=Snyder |editor2-first=C.R.}}
|2={{cite book |last1=Emmons |first1=Robert A. |chapter=Gratitude. |date=2019 |chapter-url=https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2019-20160-020 |title=Positive psychological assessment: A handbook of models and measures (2nd ed.). |pages=317–332 |editor-last=Gallagher |editor-first=Matthew W. |place=Washington |publisher=American Psychological Association |language=en |doi=10.1037/0000138-020 |isbn=978-1-4338-3107-2 |access-date=2023-02-10 |last2=Froh |first2=Jeffrey |last3=Rose |first3=Rachel |s2cid=239359085 |editor2-last=Lopez |editor2-first=Shane J.}}
}}</ref> Three [[scale (social sciences)|scales]] have been developed to measure individual differences in gratitude, each of which assesses somewhat different conceptions.<ref name="WoodFactor">{{cite journal|last1=Wood|first1=Alex M.|last2=Maltby|first2=John|last3=Stewart|first3=Neil|last4=Joseph|first4=Stephen|year=2008|url=http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/Gratitude%20and%20appreciation%20factor%20analysis.pdf|title=Conceptualizing gratitude and appreciation as a unitary personality trait|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110928103806/http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/Gratitude%20and%20appreciation%20factor%20analysis.pdf |archive-date=28 September 2011|journal=Personality and Individual Differences|volume=44|issue=3 |pages=619–630|doi=10.1016/j.paid.2007.09.028 }}</ref> The GQ6<ref name="McCullough, M. E. 2002">{{cite journal |last1=McCullough |first1=M.E. |last2=Emmons |first2=R.A. |last3=Tsang |first3=J. |year=2002 |title=The grateful disposition: A conceptual and empirical topography |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=82 |issue=1 |pages=112–127 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.82.1.112 |pmid=11811629}}</ref> measures individual differences in how frequently and intensely people feel gratitude. The Appreciation Scale<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Adler |first1=M.G. |last2=Fagley |first2=N.S. |year=2005 |title=Appreciation: Individual differences in finding value and meaning as a unique predictor of subjective well-being |journal=Journal of Personality |volume=73 |issue=1 |pages=79–114 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-6494.2004.00305.x |pmid=15660674|s2cid=41593682 }}</ref> measures eight different aspects of gratitude: appreciation of people, possessions, the present moment, rituals, feelings of [[awe]], social comparisons, existential concerns, and behavior which expresses gratitude. The GRAT<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Watkins |first1=P.C. |last2=Woodward |first2=K. |last3=Stone |first3=T. |last4=Kolts |first4=R.L. |year=2003 |title=Gratitude and happiness: Development of a measure of gratitude, and relationships with subjective well-being |journal=Social Behavior and Personality |volume=31 |issue=5 |pages=431–451 |doi=10.2224/sbp.2003.31.5.431|doi-access=free }}</ref> assesses gratitude towards other people, gratitude towards the world in general, and a lack of resentment for what you lack. A study showed that these scales all measure the same way of approaching life; this suggests that individual differences in gratitude include all of these components.<ref name="WoodFactor"/>

==Empirical findings==

===Association with well-being===
[[Image:Worshipping-god-2101347.jpg|thumb|350px|Happiness and gratitude]]
Gratitude not only contributes to positive emotions, but it also leads to a reduction in negative emotions.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Sheldon|first1=Kennon M.|last2=Kashdan|first2=Todd B.|last3=Steger|first3=Michael F. |title=Designing Positive Psychology: Taking Stock and Moving Forward |date=31 January 2011 |page=249 |isbn=978-0-19-045187-5 |publisher=Oxford University Press|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_UbiBwAAQBAJ&pg=PA248}}</ref> People who are more grateful have higher levels of [[subjective well-being]]. Grateful people are happier, less depressed, less stressed,<ref>{{Cite journal|first1=D.R.|first2=J.S.|last1=Cregg|last2= Cheavens|year=2020|title=Gratitude Interventions: Effective Self-help? A Meta-analysis of the Impact on Symptoms of Depression and Anxiety|journal=Journal of Happiness Studies|volume=22|pages=413–445|doi=10.1007/s10902-020-00236-6|s2cid=214194583}}</ref> and more satisfied with their lives and [[social relationship]]s.<ref name="McCullough, M. E. 2002"/><ref name="WoodSatisfaction">{{cite journal|last1=Wood|first1=A.M.|last2=Joseph|first2=S.|last3=Maltby|first3=J.|year=2008|url=http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/gratitude%20and%20life%20satisfaction.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110928103827/http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/gratitude%20and%20life%20satisfaction.pdf |archive-date=28 September 2011 |title=Gratitude uniquely predicts satisfaction with life: Incremental validity above the domains and facets of the Five-Factor Model|journal=Personality and Individual Differences|volume=45|pages=49–54|doi=10.1016/j.paid.2008.02.019 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Kashdan|first1=T.B.|last2=Uswatte|first2=G.|last3=Julian|first3=T.|year=2006|title=Gratitude and hedonic and eudaimonic well-being in Vietnam War veterans|journal=Behaviour Research and Therapy|volume=44|issue=2 |pages=177–199|doi=10.1016/j.brat.2005.01.005 |pmid=16389060 }}</ref> Gratitude may shield against depression by enhancing the encoding and recall of positive experiences.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Watkins|first1=P.C.|last2=Woodward|first2=K.|last3=Stone|first3=T.|last4=Kolts|first4=R.L.|year=2003|title=Gratitude and Happiness: Development of a Measure of Gratitude, and Relationships with Subjective Well-Being|journal=Social Behavior & Personality|volume=31|number=5|page=431|doi=10.2224/sbp.2003.31.5.431 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Grateful people tend to exercise greater levels of control over their environments, experience [[personal growth]], find [[Purpose of life|purpose]] in life, and [[self-acceptance|accept themselves]]. They also tend to employ more constructive coping strategies when faced with life's challenges. They are more likely to seek support from other people and to reinterpret and grow from experiences, and they spend more time planning how to deal with problems.<ref name="WoodEudamonic">{{cite journal|last1=Wood|first1=A.M.|last2=Joseph|first2=S.|last3=Maltby|first3=John|year=2009|url=http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/gratitude%20and%20psychological%20well-being.pdf|title=Gratitude predicts psychological well-being above the Big Five facets|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110928103847/http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/gratitude%20and%20psychological%20well-being.pdf |archive-date=28 September 2011|journal=Personality and Individual Differences|volume=45|pages=655–660}}</ref><ref name="WoodCoping">{{cite journal|last1=Wood|first1=A.M.|last2=Joseph|first2=S.|last3=Linley|first3=P.A.|year=2007|url=http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/gratitude%20and%20coping.pdf|title=Coping style as a psychological resource of grateful people|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110928104000/http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/gratitude%20and%20coping.pdf |archive-date=28 September 2011|journal=Journal of Social and Clinical Psychology|volume=26|issue=9 |pages=1108–1125|doi=10.1521/jscp.2007.26.9.1076 |hdl=1893/12157 }}</ref> Grateful people rely less on negative coping mechanisms, such as avoidance, self-blame, or substance use.<ref name="WoodCoping"/> Grateful people also sleep better, because they think more positive thoughts just before going to sleep.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Wood|first1=A.M.|last2=Joseph|first2=S.|last3=Lloyd|first3=J.|last4=Atkins|first4=S.|year=2009|url=http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/gratitude_sleep.pdf|title=Gratitude influences sleep through the mechanism of pre-sleep cognitions|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110928104025/http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/gratitude_sleep.pdf |archive-date=28 September 2011|journal=Journal of Psychosomatic Research|volume=66|issue=1 |pages=43–48|doi=10.1016/j.jpsychores.2008.09.002 |pmid=19073292 }}</ref> They tend to have better relationships, as a person's gratitude can positively impact their partner's satisfaction in the relationship.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Park|first1=Yoobin|last2=Impett|first2=Emily A.|last3=MacDonald|first3=Geoff|last4=Lemay|first4=Edward P. Jr.|date=31 January 2019|title=Saying 'thank you': Partners' expressions of gratitude protect relationship satisfaction and commitment from the harmful effects of attachment insecurity|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=117 |issue=4 |pages=773–806 |doi=10.1037/pspi0000178|pmid=30702317 |s2cid=73413341 }}</ref> Grateful people are more likely to have higher levels of happiness and lower levels of stress and depression.<ref name="Gratitude - Parent of all virtues"/><ref name="WoodPsychologist">{{cite journal |last1=Wood |first1=A. M. |last2=Joseph |first2=S. |last3=Linley |first3=P. A. |year=2007 |title=Gratitude: The parent of all virtues |url=http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/gratitude%20psychologist.pdf |journal=The Psychologist |volume=20 |pages=18–21 |access-date=17 January 2009 |archive-date=31 July 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150731031243/http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/gratitude%20psychologist.pdf }}</ref><ref name="McCullough, M. E. 2004">{{cite journal |last1=McCullough |first1=M. E. |last2=Tsang |first2=J. |last3=Emmons |first3=R.A. |year=2004 |title=Gratitude in Intermediate affective terrain: Links of grateful moods with individual differences and daily emotional experience |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=86 |issue=2 |pages=295–309 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.86.2.295 |pmid=14769085}}</ref>

Although many emotions and personality traits are important to well-being and [[mental health]], gratitude may be especially important. A longitudinal study showed that people who were more grateful coped better with a life transition. Specifically, people who were more grateful before the transition were less stressed, less depressed, and more satisfied with their relationships three months later.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Wood |first1=A.M. |last2=Maltby |first2=J. |last3=Gillett |first3=R. |last4=Linley |first4=P.A. |last5=Joseph |first5=S. |year=2008 |title=The role of gratitude in the development of social support, stress, and depression: Two longitudinal studies |url=http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/gratitude%20longitudinal.pdf |journal=Journal of Research in Personality |volume=42 |issue=4 |pages=854–871 |doi=10.1016/j.jrp.2007.11.003 |access-date=17 January 2009 |archive-date=28 September 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110928104039/http://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/alex.wood/gratitude%20longitudinal.pdf }}</ref> Two other studies suggested that gratitude may have a unique relationship with well-being and can explain aspects of well-being that other personality traits cannot. Both studies showed that gratitude was able to explain more well-being than the [[Big five personality traits|Big Five]] and 30 of the most commonly studied personality traits.<ref name="WoodSatisfaction"/><ref name="WoodEudamonic"/>

Gratitude also has a positive impact on physical well-being.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last1=Fritz|first1=Megan M.|last2=Armenta|first2=Christina N.|last3=Walsh|first3=Lisa C.|last4=Lyubomirsky|first4=Sonja|date=March 2019|title=Gratitude facilitates healthy eating behavior in adolescents and young adults|journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology|volume=81|pages=4–14|doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2018.08.011|s2cid=52241900|issn=0022-1031|url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0022103117308569|url-access=subscription}}</ref> For example, in one study, teens who wrote letters expressing gratitude to other people over the course of a month were more inclined to eat healthier food.<ref name=":1"/> This phenomenon might be explained by the notion that when people experience gratitude, they are more motivated to reciprocate the kindness shown by others. Therefore, rather than engaging in behaviors that may undermine their own health, they feel driven to adopt healthier lifestyles as a way of acknowledging the support they have received from others. Moreover, gratitude is known to trigger positive emotions, which in turn direct individuals' attention towards optimistic possibilities in the future. As a result, people are more likely to embrace behaviors that are conducive to a better future, such as healthy eating.<ref name=":1"/>

People who express gratitude also demonstrate improved overall health by way of greater physical activity, better sleep, fewer health care visits, and better nutrition.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Croke |first=Lisa |date=29 October 2019 |title=Cultivating gratitude can lead to mental, physical, and work-related benefits |journal=AORN Journal |volume=110 |issue=5 |pages=P5 |doi=10.1002/aorn.12871 |pmid=31660603 |s2cid=204952967|doi-access=free }}</ref> Practicing gratitude may be correlated with small improvements in cardiovascular health.<ref>{{multiref2
|1={{Cite journal|last1=Redwine|first1=Laura S.|last2=Henry|first2=Brook L.|last3=Pung|first3=Meredith A.|last4=Wilson|first4=Kathleen|last5=Chinh|first5=Kelly|last6=Knight|first6=Brian|last7=Jain|first7=Shamini|last8=Rutledge|first8=Thomas|last9=Greenberg|first9=Barry|last10=Maisel|first10=Alan|last11=Mills|first11=Paul J.|date=July 2016|title=Pilot Randomized Study of a Gratitude Journaling Intervention on Heart Rate Variability and Inflammatory Biomarkers in Patients With Stage B Heart Failure|journal=Psychosomatic Medicine|volume=78|issue=6|pages=667–676|doi=10.1097/psy.0000000000000316|pmid=27187845|issn=1534-7796|pmc=4927423}}
|2={{Cite journal|last1=Mills|first1=Paul J.|last2=Redwine|first2=Laura|last3=Wilson|first3=Kathleen|last4=Pung|first4=Meredith A.|last5=Chinh|first5=Kelly|last6=Greenberg|first6=Barry H.|last7=Lunde|first7=Ottar|last8=Maisel|first8=Alan|last9=Raisinghani|first9=Ajit|last10=Wood|first10=Alex|last11=Chopra|first11=Deepak|date=March 2015|title=The role of gratitude in spiritual well-being in asymptomatic heart failure patients.|journal=Spirituality in Clinical Practice|volume=2|issue=1|pages=5–17|doi=10.1037/scp0000050|pmid=26203459|issn=2326-4519|pmc=4507265}}
|3={{Cite journal|last1=Gallagher|first1=Stephen|last2=Solano|first2=Alejandro Castro|last3=Liporace|first3=Mercedes Fernández|date=July 2020|title=State, but not trait gratitude is associated with cardiovascular responses to acute psychological stress|url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0031938420302134|journal=Physiology & Behavior|volume=221|article-number=112896|doi=10.1016/j.physbeh.2020.112896|pmid=32251630|issn=0031-9384|hdl=10344/8896|s2cid=214788940|hdl-access=free|url-access=subscription}} }}</ref>

===Relationship to altruism===
Gratitude makes people more altruistic. One study found that gratitude correlates with economic generosity.<ref name=":0">{{cite journal|pmid=20364907|year=2010|last1=Desteno|first1=D.|title=Gratitude as moral sentiment: Emotion-guided cooperation in economic exchange|journal=Emotion|volume=10|issue=2|pages=289–93|last2=Bartlett|first2=M.Y.|last3=Baumann|first3=J.|last4=Williams|first4=L.A.|last5=Dickens|first5=L.|doi=10.1037/a0017883}}</ref> The study used an economic game, and showed increased gratitude to directly mediate increased monetary giving, and that grateful people are more likely to sacrifice individual gains for communal profit. Another study found similar correlations between gratitude and empathy, generosity, and helpfulness towards the creation of social reciprocity, even with strangers, that is beneficial to the individuals in the short and in the middle terms.{{r|Highlights}}

===As a motivator of behavior===
{{see also|Ben Franklin effect}}
Gratitude may reinforce future prosocial behavior in benefactors. For example, one experiment found that customers of a jewelry store who were called and thanked showed a subsequent 70% increase in purchases. In comparison, customers who were called and told about a sale showed only a 30% increase in purchases, while customers who were not called at all showed no increase in purchases.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Carey |first1=J.R. |last2=Clicque |first2=S.H. |last3=Leighton |first3=B.A. |last4=Milton |first4=F. |year=1976 |title=A test of positive reinforcement of customers |journal=Journal of Marketing |volume=40 |issue=4 |pages=98–100 |doi=10.1177/002224297604000413 |s2cid=167486513}}</ref> In another study, a restaurant's regular patrons gave bigger [[tip (gratuity)|tips]] when servers wrote "Thank you" on their checks.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rind |first1=B. |last2=Bordia |first2=P. |year=1995 |title=Effect of server's "Thank you" and personalization on restaurant tipping |journal=Journal of Applied Social Psychology |volume=25 |issue=9 |pages=745–751 |doi=10.1111/j.1559-1816.1995.tb01772.x}}</ref>

Some{{who|date=March 2023}} starkly distinguish between gratitude and [[indebtedness]]. While both [[emotions]] may occur in response to help or favors, indebtedness is said to occur when an individual subjectively perceives that they are under an obligation to provide repayment or compensation for the aid.<ref>{{cite book|last=Greenberg|first=M.S.|year=1980|chapter=A theory of indebtedness|editor-first1=K.J.|editor-last1=Gergen|editor-first2=M.S.|editor-last2=Greenberg|editor-first3=R.H.|editor-last3=Wills|title=Social exchange: Advances in theory and research|location=New York|publisher=Plenum}}</ref> The two emotions then lead to different actions: Indebtedness may motivate the recipient to avoid the person who helped them, whereas gratitude may motivate a recipient to seek out their benefactor and to improve their relationship with them.<ref>{{multiref2
|1={{cite journal |last1 = Watkins |first1 = P.C. |last2 = Scheer |first2 = J. |last3 = Ovnicek |first3 = M. |last4 = Kolts |first4 = R. |year = 2006 |title = The debt of gratitude: Dissociating gratitude and indebtedness |journal = Cognition and Emotion |volume = 20 |issue = 2 |pages = 217–241 |doi=10.1080/02699930500172291 |s2cid=145350764}}
|2=Tsang, J.A. (2006).{{full citation needed|date=July 2023}}}}</ref> A study of the feelings of migrant adolescents towards their parents noted that "gratitude serves and indebtedness challenges intergenerational relations after migration". The study also noted that, "when the expectations of return from the benefactor increase, indebtedness of the beneficiary increases but gratitude decreases".<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Turjanmaa|first1=Elina|last2=Jasinskaja-Lahti|first2=Inga|year=2020|title=Thanks but No Thanks? Gratitude and Indebtedness Within Intergenerational Relations After Immigration|url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/fare.12401|journal=Family Relations|language=en|volume=69|issue=1|pages=63–75|doi=10.1111/fare.12401|hdl=10138/322006|s2cid=210539269|issn=1741-3729|hdl-access=free}}</ref> Unlike compassion or sadness, gratitude decreases [[Nicotine dependence|cigarette craving]] suggesting a potential role in public health reductions of appetitive risk behaviors.<ref name="r756">{{cite journal |last1=Wang |first1=Ke |last2=Rees |first2=Vaughan W. |last3=Dorison |first3=Charles A. |last4=Kawachi |first4=Ichiro |last5=Lerner |first5=Jennifer S. |date=2024-07-09 |title=The role of positive emotion in harmful health behavior: Implications for theory and public health campaigns |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |volume=121 |issue=28 |article-number=e2320750121 |doi=10.1073/pnas.2320750121 |issn=0027-8424|doi-access=free |pmid=38950367 |bibcode=2024PNAS..12120750W |pmc=11252813 }}</ref>

===Psychological interventions===
[[Image:Gratitude journal.jpg|thumb|200px|A gratitude journal is a means of learning to focus on things that make one grateful, which can enhance happiness.]]
Several psychological interventions have been developed to increase gratitude.<ref name="Gratitude - Parent of all virtues"/><ref name="WoodPsychologist"/><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Emmons|first1=R.A.|last2=McCullough|first2=M.E.|year=2003|title=Counting blessings versus burdens: An experimental investigation of gratitude and subjective well-being in daily life|journal=[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]|volume=84|issue=2 |pages=377–389|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.84.2.377 |pmid=12585811 |s2cid=10482709 |url=https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/pdfs/GratitudePDFs/6Emmons-BlessingsBurdens.pdf}}</ref>

For example, Watkins and colleagues<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Watkins |first1=P.C. |last2=Woodward |first2=K. |last3=Stone |first3=T. |last4=Kolts |first4=R.L. |year=2003 |title=Gratitude and happiness: Development of a measure of gratitude, and relationships with subjective well-being |journal=Social Behavior and Personality |volume=31 |issue=5 |pages=431–452 |doi=10.2224/sbp.2003.31.5.431|doi-access=free }}</ref> had participants test a number of different gratitude exercises, such as thinking about a living person for whom they are grateful, writing about someone for whom they are grateful, and writing a letter to deliver to someone for whom they are grateful. Participants in the control group were asked to describe their living room. Participants who engaged in a gratitude exercise showed increases in their experiences of positive emotion immediately after the exercise, and this effect was strongest for participants who were asked to think about a person for whom they are grateful. Participants who had grateful personalities to begin with showed the greatest benefit from these gratitude exercises.

In another study, participants were randomly assigned to one of six therapeutic intervention conditions designed to improve the participants' overall quality of life.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Seligman |first1=M.E.P. |last2=Steen |first2=T.A. |last3=Park |first3=N. |last4=Peterson |first4=C. |year=2005 |title=Positive psychology progress: Empirical validation of interventions |journal=American Psychologist |volume=60 |issue=5 |pages=410–421 |doi=10.1037/0003-066x.60.5.410 |pmid=16045394|s2cid=6262678 }}</ref> Of these, the biggest short-term effects came from a "gratitude visit" in which participants wrote and delivered a letter of gratitude to someone in their life. This condition showed a rise in happiness scores by 10 percent and a significant fall in depression scores, results that lasted up to one month after the visit. Of the six conditions, the longest-lasting effects were associated with writing "[[gratitude journal]]s" of three things they were grateful for every day. These participants' happiness scores also increased and continued to increase each time they were tested periodically after the experiment. The greatest benefits were usually found to occur around six months after treatment began. This exercise was so successful that although participants were only asked to continue the journal for a week, many participants continued to keep the journal long after the study was over.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Seligman |first1=Martin E.P. |last2=Steen |first2=Tracy A. |last3=Park |first3=Nansook |last4=Peterson |first4=Christopher |date=2005 |title=Positive Psychology Progress: Empirical Validation of Interventions. |url=https://psycnet.apa.org/doiLanding?doi=10.1037%2F0003-066X.60.5.410 |journal=American Psychologist |language=en |volume=60 |issue=5 |pages=410–421 |doi=10.1037/0003-066X.60.5.410 |pmid=16045394 |s2cid=6262678 |issn=1935-990X|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Similar results have been found in studies conducted by Emmons and McCullough (2003)<ref name="McCullough, M.E. 2002"/> and Lyubomirsky ''et al.'' (2005).<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Lyubomirsky |first1=Sonja |last2=Sheldon |first2=Kennon M. |last3=Schkade |first3=David |date=2005 |title=Pursuing Happiness: The Architecture of Sustainable Change |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1037/1089-2680.9.2.111 |journal=Review of General Psychology |language=en |volume=9 |issue=2 |pages=111–131 |doi=10.1037/1089-2680.9.2.111 |issn=1089-2680|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

The [[Greater Good Science Center]] at the [[University of California, Berkeley]], offered awards for dissertation-level research projects with the greatest potential to advance the science and practice of gratitude.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://ggsc.berkeley.edu/what_we_do/major_initiatives/expanding_gratitude/gratitude_research_grant_winners/dissertationaward|title=Gratitude Dissertation Fellows|website=Greater Good Science Center}}</ref>

=== Relationship to mental health ===
A study on the benefits of mental health counseling<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Wong|first1=Y. Joel|last2=Owen|first2=Jesse|last3=Gabana|first3=Nicole T.|last4=Brown|first4=Joshua W.|last5=McInnis|first5=Sydney|last6=Toth|first6=Paul|last7=Gilman|first7=Lynn|date=4 March 2018|title=Does gratitude writing improve the mental health of psychotherapy clients? Evidence from a randomized controlled trial|url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/10503307.2016.1169332|journal=Psychotherapy Research|volume=28|issue=2|pages=192–202|doi=10.1080/10503307.2016.1169332|issn=1050-3307|pmid=27139595|s2cid=5892909|url-access=subscription}}</ref> divided approximately 300 college students into three groups prior to their first counseling session. The first group was instructed to write one letter of gratitude a week for three weeks, the second group was asked to write about their negative experiences, and the third group received only counseling. When compared, the first group reported better mental health after completing their writing exercises. The study suggests that practicing gratitude may help the brain react more sensitively to the experience of gratitude in the future, and therefore, may improve mental health.<ref>{{Cite journal|first1=Joshua|last1=Brown|first2=Joel|last2=Wong|date=6 June 2017|title=How Gratitude Changes You and Your Brain|url=https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/article/item/how_gratitude_changes_you_and_your_brain|access-date=11 April 2021|journal=Greater Good Magazine|language=en}}</ref>

==Conclusions==
{{quote|Benevolence gladdens constantly the grateful; the ungrateful, however, but once.|Seneca<ref>[[Lucius Annaeus Seneca]] (the Younger), ''De Beneficiis'' book III, section XVII</ref>}}
{{quote|Gratitude is not only the greatest of virtues, but the parent of all others.|Cicero}}

Multiple studies have shown the positive correlation between gratitude and increased well being, not only for the person expressing gratitude, but for all people involved.<ref name="McCullough, M. E. 2004"/><ref name=":0"/> The positive psychology movement embraced these studies and, in an effort to increase overall well-being, has begun to incorporate mental and emotional exercises to increase gratitude.<ref>{{Cite journal|first=G.W.|last=Hohmann|date=1966|journal=Psychophysiology|volume=3|issue=2|pages=143–156|doi=10.1111/j.1469-8986.1966.tb02690.x|title=Some Effects of Spinal Cord Lesions on Experienced Emotional Feelings|pmid=5927829}}</ref>

==Further reading==
{{Library resources box}}
* {{cite book |title=Emotional Success: The Power of Gratitude, Compassion, and Pride |first=David |last=DeSteno |isbn=978-0-544-70310-0 |year=2018 |publisher=Eamon Dolan/Houghton Mifflin Harcourt}} [https://books.google.com/books?id=l99DDwAAQBAJ Description] & [https://books.google.com/books?id=l99DDwAAQBAJ&pg=PR7 contents], summarizing scientific studies.
* {{cite book | last=Emmons | first=Robert A. | title=The Little Book of Gratitude: Create a Life of Happiness and Well-Being By Giving Thanks | publisher=Gaia|location=London | year=2016 | isbn=978-1-85675-365-4}}
* {{cite book | last=Emmons | first=Robert A. | title=Gratitude Works! A Twenty-One-Day Program for Creating Emotional Prosperity| publisher=John Wiley & Sons |location=San Francisco | year=2013 | isbn=978-1-118-13129-9}}
* {{cite book | last=Emmons | first=Robert A. | title=Thanks! How the New Science of Gratitude Can Make You Happier | publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt |location=Boston, Mass. | year=2007 | isbn=978-0-618-62019-7}}
* {{cite book | last1=Emmons | first1= R.A.|last2=Hill|first2=J.|year=2001|title=Words of gratitude for mind, body, and soul|location=Radnor, Penn.|publisher=Templeton Foundation Press}}
* {{cite book | last=Emmons | first=Robert A. | title=The Psychology of Ultimate Concerns: Motivation and spirituality in personality | location=New York|publisher=Guilford Press | year=1999 | isbn=978-1-57230-456-7}}
* {{cite journal |title=How to Savor Gratitude and Disarm 'Gratitude Traps' |last=Kenner |first=Ellen |journal=The Objective Standard |volume=15 |issue= 2 |pages=13–22 |publisher=Glen Allen Press |year= 2020 }}
* {{cite book | last=Nelson | first=Kristi | title=Wake Up Grateful: The Transformative Practice of Taking Nothing for Granted | publisher=Storey Publishing, LLC | year=2020 | isbn=978-1-63586-244-7}}
